result.markdown:
# Topic 3 — Further Exercises {.unnumbered} These exercises give you an opportunity to consolidate everything covered in Topic 3 before moving on. Unlike the exercises after each video, many of these combine skills from several videos into a single task. Some exercises also introduce new concepts that extend what you have learned. The exercises are organised into three levels: - 🟢 **Warm-up** — single-skill exercises to check your understanding of the basics. - 🟡 **Practice** — exercises that combine two or more topics. - 🔴 **Challenge** — multi-step tasks that may require you to combine ideas in ways you have not seen before. Some may require looking things up or experimenting. --- ## 🟢 Warm-up ### Exercise 3.11.1 Use the `titanic` dataset to answer the following questions using groupby and aggregation. Each question requires a single groupby operation. ```python import pandas as pd titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") ``` 1. Compute the mean and median fare paid by passengers in each embarkation port (`embarked`). Which port has the highest mean fare? Is the mean or median more representative here, and why? 2. For each combination of passenger class and sex, compute the survival rate and the number of passengers. Sort the result by survival rate in descending order. 3. Compute the mean age and mean fare for each passenger class.
Solution ::: {#b495f6d6 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") # 1. Mean and median fare by embarkation port print( titanic.groupby("embarked")["fare"] .agg(mean_fare="mean", median_fare="median") .round(2) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean_fare median_fare embarked C 59.95 29.70 Q 13.28 7.75 S 27.08 13.00 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#a44ae645 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Survival rate and count by class and sex print( titanic.groupby(["pclass", "sex"]) .agg(survival_rate=("survived", "mean"), n=("survived", "count")) .round(3) .sort_values("survival_rate", ascending=False) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` survival_rate n pclass sex 1 female 0.968 94 2 female 0.921 76 3 female 0.500 144 1 male 0.369 122 2 male 0.157 108 3 male 0.135 347 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#e26a7e14 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Mean age and fare by passenger class print(titanic.groupby("pclass")[["fare", "age"]].mean().round(1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` fare age pclass 1 84.2 38.2 2 20.7 29.9 3 13.7 25.1 ``` ::: ::: 1. Port C (Cherbourg) has the highest mean fare by a considerable margin. The mean is likely less representative than the median here because fare distributions tend to be right-skewed — a small number of first-class passengers paying very high fares pulls the mean upward. 2. First-class female passengers have the highest survival rate. The pattern is consistent: within each class, female passengers survived at much higher rates than male passengers, and within each sex, higher classes survived at higher rates. 3. Both the average age and fare are increasing with class. Note that because the same aggregation (mean) is computed on the two columns, it is not necessary to use `agg()`.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.2 Use the `healthexp` dataset to practice filtering and sorting. Each task below requires a single filter or sort operation. ```python import pandas as pd healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") ``` 1. Filter to observations where life expectancy is above 80 and health spending is below 5 000 USD. Which country-year combinations meet both criteria? 2. Sort the dataset by life expectancy in descending order and print the top 10 rows. Which country and year holds the record for highest life expectancy in the dataset? 3. Filter to the most recent year available and sort by spending in ascending order. Which country spends the least on health in the most recent year?
Solution ::: {#043f746f .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") # 1. Life expectancy above 80 and spending below 5000 efficient = healthexp[ (healthexp["Life_Expectancy"] > 80) & (healthexp["Spending_USD"] < 5_000) ] print(efficient[["Country", "Year", "Life_Expectancy", "Spending_USD"]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Country Year Life_Expectancy Spending_USD 128 Japan 1996 80.3 1436.372 134 Japan 1997 80.5 1529.586 140 Japan 1998 80.6 1571.107 146 Japan 1999 80.5 1667.922 152 Japan 2000 81.2 1847.786 .. ... ... ... ... 259 Great Britain 2018 81.3 4189.708 260 Japan 2018 84.3 4554.276 265 Great Britain 2019 81.4 4385.463 266 Japan 2019 84.4 4610.794 272 Japan 2020 84.7 4665.641 [66 rows x 4 columns] ``` ::: ::: ::: {#c8369880 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Sort by life expectancy descending, top 10 print( healthexp.sort_values("Life_Expectancy", ascending=False) .head(10)[["Country", "Year", "Life_Expectancy"]] ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Country Year Life_Expectancy 272 Japan 2020 84.7 266 Japan 2019 84.4 260 Japan 2018 84.3 254 Japan 2017 84.2 248 Japan 2016 84.1 242 Japan 2015 83.9 236 Japan 2014 83.7 230 Japan 2013 83.4 224 Japan 2012 83.2 206 Japan 2009 83.0 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#06a1e959 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Most recent year, sorted by spending ascending latest = healthexp[healthexp["Year"] == healthexp["Year"].max()] print( latest.sort_values("Spending_USD") [["Country", "Year", "Spending_USD", "Life_Expectancy"]] ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Country Year Spending_USD Life_Expectancy 272 Japan 2020 4665.641 84.7 271 Great Britain 2020 5018.700 80.4 270 France 2020 5468.418 82.3 268 Canada 2020 5828.324 81.7 269 Germany 2020 6938.983 81.1 273 USA 2020 11859.179 77.0 ``` ::: ::: Japan appears frequently in all groups — achieving high life expectancy with relatively modest spending compared to other high-income countries. This is the same efficiency pattern explored in the capstone video.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.3 Use the `flights` dataset to practice working with dates. Start by building the date column as shown in the video. ```python import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) ``` 1. Extract the month number from the `date` column as a new column `month_num`. Then compute the mean number of passengers for each month number across all years. Which month number has the highest average passenger count? 2. Filter the dataset to all observations from the second half of the year (July through December) and compute the total passengers in this period. Compare this to the total for the first half of the year. 3. Use `.dt.day_name()` to add a column `day_name` showing the name of the day of the week each date falls on. Which day of the week do the most flight dates in the dataset fall on, and why does this make sense given the resolution of the data?
Solution ::: {#3767f877 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) # 1. Mean passengers by month number flights["month_num"] = flights["date"].dt.month print(flights.groupby("month_num")["passengers"].mean().round(1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` month_num 1 241.8 2 235.0 3 270.2 4 267.1 5 271.8 6 311.7 7 351.3 8 351.1 9 302.4 10 266.6 11 232.8 12 261.8 Name: passengers, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#2207b937 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. First vs second half of the year first_half = flights[flights["month_num"] <= 6]["passengers"].sum() second_half = flights[flights["month_num"] > 6]["passengers"].sum() print(f"First half: {first_half:,}") print(f"Second half: {second_half:,}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` First half: 19,170 Second half: 21,193 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#a5719f67 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Day of the week flights["day_name"] = flights["date"].dt.day_name() print(flights["day_name"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` day_name Tuesday 23 Friday 21 Sunday 21 Thursday 21 Saturday 20 Wednesday 19 Monday 19 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 1. Month 7 (July) has the highest average passenger count, consistent with summer being the peak travel season. 2. The second half of the year has more total passengers than the first half, driven by the summer peak in July through September. 3. All dates fall on the first day of the month. Because `pd.to_datetime()` with a `%Y-%b` format string sets the day to 1 by default, every date in the dataset is the first of a month. The day of the week on which the first of the month falls depends on the year and month, so the distribution across weekdays is roughly even — but all values represent the same type of date (first of month), not randomly distributed days.
--- ## 🟡 Practice ### Exercise 3.11.4 This exercise combines filtering, missing value handling, column creation, and groupby to produce a summary of Titanic survival patterns. ```python import pandas as pd titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") ``` 1. Drop rows where `embarked` is missing. Then fill missing values in `age` with the median age within each passenger class using `groupby` and `transform`. Verify that no missing values remain in either column. 2. Create a column `age_group` with the following categories: - `"child"` if age is below 18 - `"adult"` if age is between 18 and 59 - `"senior"` if age is 60 or above 3. Compute the survival rate for each combination of `age_group` and `pclass`. Sort by survival rate in descending order. Which group had the highest survival rate? 4. Compute the share of each `age_group` within each passenger class. Does the age distribution differ meaningfully across classes?
Hint For step 2, you could write a function with an `if/elif/else` chain and use `.apply()`. But pandas also has a vectorised function called `np.select()` that handles exactly this kind of multi-branch classification without needing a custom function. It takes a list of conditions and a matching list of values: ```python conditions = [condition1, condition2] choices = [value1, value2] np.select(conditions, choices, default=default_value) ``` You can look up the documentation for `np.select()` by running `help(np.select)` in the console or searching online.
Solution ::: {#2f287dca .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") # 1. Drop missing embarked, fill missing age by class titanic = titanic.dropna(subset=["embarked"]) titanic["age"] = titanic["age"].fillna( titanic.groupby("pclass")["age"].transform("median") ) print(titanic[["age", "embarked"]].isna().sum()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` age 0 embarked 0 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#f385eef3 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Create age_group conditions = [titanic["age"] < 18, titanic["age"] < 60] choices = ["child", "adult"] titanic["age_group"] = np.select(conditions, choices, default="senior") print(titanic["age_group"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` age_group adult 751 child 113 senior 25 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#88a585db .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Survival rate by age group and class print( titanic.groupby(["age_group", "pclass"])["survived"] .mean() .round(3) .sort_values(ascending=False) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` age_group pclass child 1 0.917 2 0.913 adult 1 0.640 2 0.414 child 3 0.372 senior 2 0.250 1 0.250 adult 3 0.218 senior 3 0.200 Name: survived, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#3bc4eaeb .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Share of each age group within each class age_class = titanic.groupby(["pclass", "age_group"]).size() age_class_share = (age_class / titanic.groupby("pclass")["age_group"].count()).round(3) print(age_class_share) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` pclass age_group 1 adult 0.869 child 0.056 senior 0.075 2 adult 0.853 child 0.125 senior 0.022 3 adult 0.831 child 0.159 senior 0.010 dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: 3. First-class children had the highest survival rate. In general, children and first-class passengers survived at higher rates. The "women and children first" evacuation policy is clearly visible in the data. 4. Third-class passengers tend to be younger on average than first-class passengers, with a higher share of children and young adults — consistent with the historical pattern of younger emigrants travelling in lower classes. Note the difference between `.size()` and `.count()` used above. `.size()` is applied directly to the grouped object and counts the number of rows in each group, including rows with missing values. `.count()` is applied to a specific column and counts only the non-missing values in that column. This also means `.size()` can be used to count rows even when you have not selected a column. Since `age_group` has no missing values here, the two approaches give the same result — but with a column that does have missing values, `.count()` would return a smaller number than `.size()`.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.5 This exercise asks you to implement the percentage change calculation two ways: first with a manual loop, and then with a pandas built-in function. The goal is to make a habit of checking whether pandas already has a function for what you want to do, before writing your own. ```python import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) flights = flights.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True) ``` **Part 1.** Without using any pandas function designed for this purpose, compute the month-on-month percentage change in `passengers` using a `for` loop. For each row (starting from the second row), use `.iloc[]` to access the current and previous row's passenger count, compute the percentage change using the formula below, and store the result in a new column `pct_change_manual`. The first row should be `NaN` since there is no previous value to compare against. $$\text{percentage change} = \frac{\text{current} - \text{previous}}{\text{previous}} \times 100$$ **Part 2.** pandas has a built-in method that computes percentage change directly on a Series in a single line. Look up the documentation for `pd.Series.pct_change()` and use it to create a second column `pct_change_pandas`. Multiply by 100 to match the scale of Part 1. **Part 3.** Drop rows where the percentage change is missing, and verify that the two columns are identical. Then filter to rows where the month-on-month change exceeds 20% and print those rows. In which years and months does passenger growth spike most strongly?
Hint For Part 1, initialize the new column with `NaN` for every row, for example using `flights["pct_change_manual"] = None`. Then loop over the row positions with `for i in range(1, len(flights))`, and use `.iloc[]` to access the current row (`flights.iloc[i]`) and the previous row (`flights.iloc[i - 1]`). Assign the computed value back into the column at position `i` using `flights.loc[i, "pct_change_manual"] = ...`.
Solution ::: {#ece7f6de .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) flights = flights.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True) # Part 1: manual loop flights["pct_change_manual"] = None for i in range(1, len(flights)): current = flights.iloc[i]["passengers"] previous = flights.iloc[i - 1]["passengers"] flights.loc[i, "pct_change_manual"] = (current - previous) / previous * 100 flights["pct_change_manual"] = flights["pct_change_manual"].astype(float) # Part 2: pandas built-in flights["pct_change_pandas"] = flights["passengers"].pct_change() * 100 flights[["date", "passengers", "pct_change_manual", "pct_change_pandas"]].head(10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ```{=html}
date passengers pct_change_manual pct_change_pandas
0 1949-01-01 112 NaN NaN
1 1949-02-01 118 5.357143 5.357143
2 1949-03-01 132 11.864407 11.864407
3 1949-04-01 129 -2.272727 -2.272727
4 1949-05-01 121 -6.201550 -6.201550
5 1949-06-01 135 11.570248 11.570248
6 1949-07-01 148 9.629630 9.629630
7 1949-08-01 148 0.000000 0.000000
8 1949-09-01 136 -8.108108 -8.108108
9 1949-10-01 119 -12.500000 -12.500000
``` ::: ::: ::: {#c877b46c .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Part 3: verify and filter to spikes above 20% flights = flights.dropna() print((flights["pct_change_manual"].round(6) == flights["pct_change_pandas"].round(6)).all()) spikes = flights[flights["pct_change_pandas"] > 20] print(spikes[["date", "passengers", "pct_change_pandas"]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` True date passengers pct_change_pandas 23 1950-12-01 140 22.807018 50 1953-03-01 236 20.408163 62 1954-03-01 235 25.000000 ``` ::: ::: The two columns are identical. A short note on the loop mechanics: initializing the column with `flights["pct_change_manual"] = None` creates an `object`-dtype column, since `None` is not numeric. Assigning floats into individual cells with `.loc[]` does not automatically upgrade the column's dtype, so `.astype(float)` is needed afterward to convert it to a proper numeric column — this also turns the remaining `None` in the first row into a genuine `NaN`. Even after this conversion, comparing the two columns with `==` will mark the first row as unequal, because `NaN == NaN` always evaluates to `False` in pandas, regardless of how the missing value was created. Alternatively you can use `.equals()` rather than `==` as this treats matching `NaN` positions as equals: ``` flights["pct_change_manual"].round(6).equals(flights["pct_change_pandas"].round(6)) ``` The broader lesson: a `for` loop with `.iloc[]` works, but it is slow, verbose, and easy to get wrong — for example by forgetting to handle the first row, or making an off-by-one error in the row indices. Before reaching for a loop, it is always worth checking whether pandas already has a built-in vectorised function for the operation. `.pct_change()`, `.cumsum()`, `.diff()`, and `.shift()` cover many common row-wise operations without needing a loop or a custom function at all.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.6 This exercise combines merging, groupby, and column creation to extend the analysis from the capstone video. Load the cleaned healthexp data and the continents table, merge them, and answer the questions below. ```python import pandas as pd healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns] healthexp["country"] = healthexp["country"].replace({ "Great Britain": "United Kingdom", "USA": "United States", }) url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv" continents = pd.read_csv(url) continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"]) continents = continents.rename(columns={ "Entity": "country", "Countries Continents": "continent" }) healthexp = healthexp.merge(continents, on="country", how="left").sort_values(["year", "country"]) ``` 1. Compute the mean life expectancy and mean spending per continent across all years. Sort by mean life expectancy in descending order. 2. For each country, compute the total growth in life expectancy from the earliest to the most recent year available. Which country improved the most? 3. Create a column `spending_vs_continent_mean` that shows how much each country-year observation's spending deviates from the mean spending of its continent in that year. Use `groupby` and `transform`. Which country-year observations have the largest positive deviation?
Hint To compute the growth from the earliest to the most recent year for each country, you need the first and last observed value within each group. Two useful groupby methods for this are `.first()` and `.last()`, which return the first and last row's value in each group respectively — for example `healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].first()` gives each country's earliest recorded life expectancy. This assumes the data is sorted by year within each country, which it already is here.
Solution ::: {#2874efff .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns] healthexp["country"] = healthexp["country"].replace({ "Great Britain": "United Kingdom", "USA": "United States", }) url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv" continents = pd.read_csv(url) continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"]) continents = continents.rename(columns={ "Entity": "country", "Countries Continents": "continent" }) healthexp = healthexp.merge(continents, on="country", how="left").sort_values(["year", "country"]) # 1. Mean life expectancy and spending by continent print( healthexp.groupby("continent") .agg(mean_life_exp=("life_expectancy", "mean"), mean_spending=("spending_usd", "mean")) .round(1) .sort_values("mean_life_exp", ascending=False) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean_life_exp mean_spending continent Asia 79.6 1860.3 Europe 77.8 2557.9 North America 77.2 3599.9 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#e2075397 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Life expectancy growth per country earliest = healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].first() latest = healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].last() growth = (latest - earliest).sort_values(ascending=False) print(growth.round(2)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` country Japan 12.7 Germany 10.5 France 10.1 Canada 8.9 United Kingdom 8.5 United States 6.1 Name: life_expectancy, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#6728f11f .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Deviation from continent mean spending by year healthexp["continent_mean_spending"] = ( healthexp.groupby(["continent", "year"])["spending_usd"] .transform("mean") ) healthexp["spending_vs_continent_mean"] = ( healthexp["spending_usd"] - healthexp["continent_mean_spending"] ).round(2) print( healthexp.sort_values("spending_vs_continent_mean", ascending=False) [["country", "year", "spending_usd", "spending_vs_continent_mean"]] .head(10) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` country year spending_usd spending_vs_continent_mean 273 United States 2020 11859.179 3015.43 267 United States 2019 10855.517 2832.90 261 United States 2018 10451.386 2571.52 255 United States 2017 10046.472 2448.00 243 United States 2015 9355.118 2359.92 249 United States 2016 9717.649 2336.69 237 United States 2014 8925.879 2194.53 231 United States 2013 8519.620 2045.43 225 United States 2012 8346.064 2004.91 219 United States 2011 8079.467 1925.25 ``` ::: ::: Japan shows among the strongest improvements in life expectancy over the period covered. The USA consistently shows the largest positive deviation from its continent's mean spending, reflecting its outlier status in health expenditure among comparable countries.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.7 This exercise combines `np.where()` and `np.select()` with groupby to classify and compare countries in the `healthexp` dataset. ```python import pandas as pd import numpy as np healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns] ``` 1. Use `np.where()` to create a column `high_life_exp` that is `1` if life expectancy is above the overall median and `0` otherwise. 2. Use `np.select()` to create a column `spending_tier` with three categories: - `"low"` if spending is below the 33rd percentile - `"medium"` if spending is between the 33rd and 66th percentile - `"high"` if spending is above the 66th percentile 3. Compute the mean life expectancy for each `spending_tier`. Does higher spending reliably predict higher life expectancy across all tiers? 4. Compute the share of observations in the `high_life_exp` group for each country. Which countries are above the median life expectancy for the largest share of their observed years?
Hint To compute the 33rd and 66th percentiles of the spending column, use `healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.33)` and `healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.66)`.
Solution ::: {#a8c46d67 .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns] # 1. High life expectancy indicator median_life = healthexp["life_expectancy"].median() healthexp["high_life_exp"] = np.where( healthexp["life_expectancy"] > median_life, 1, 0 ) print(healthexp["high_life_exp"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` high_life_exp 0 138 1 136 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#b456041e .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Spending tier p33 = healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.33) p66 = healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.66) conditions = [ healthexp["spending_usd"] < p33, healthexp["spending_usd"] < p66 ] choices = ["low", "medium"] healthexp["spending_tier"] = np.select(conditions, choices, default="high") print(healthexp["spending_tier"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` spending_tier high 93 low 91 medium 90 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#6b6b21e2 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Mean life expectancy by spending tier print( healthexp.groupby("spending_tier")["life_expectancy"] .mean() .round(2) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` spending_tier high 80.55 low 74.73 medium 78.39 Name: life_expectancy, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#d8adec6f .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Share of years above median life expectancy per country print( healthexp.groupby("country")["high_life_exp"] .mean() .sort_values(ascending=False) .round(3) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` country France 0.714 Japan 0.667 Canada 0.568 Great Britain 0.465 Germany 0.420 USA 0.216 Name: high_life_exp, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: 3. Higher spending tiers are associated with higher mean life expectancy, but the relationship is not perfectly linear. Japan achieves very high life expectancy with moderate spending — it frequently appears in the high life expectancy group despite not being in the highest spending tier. 4. Japan and France are above the global median life expectancy for all or nearly all of their observed years, while countries with lower spending histories appear above the median for a smaller share of years.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.8 This exercise explores two different ways of merging a monthly and a quarterly time series, and the different results they produce. We will use two files: - [`FRED_monthly_2010.csv`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv) — monthly US economic indicators for the 2010s: `CPI` (the Consumer Price Index, a measure of the general price level), `UNRATE` (the unemployment rate, in percent), `FEDFUNDS` (the federal funds rate, the Federal Reserve's key short-term interest rate), `REALRATE` (the federal funds rate adjusted for inflation), and `LFPART` (the labor force participation rate, the share of the working-age population that is employed or looking for work). - [`GDP.csv`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/GDP.csv) — quarterly US Gross Domestic Product, in billions of dollars. ```python import pandas as pd url_fred = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv" url_gdp = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/GDP.csv" fred = pd.read_csv(url_fred) fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"]) gdp = pd.read_csv(url_gdp) gdp["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(gdp["DATE"]) print(fred.head()) print(gdp.head()) ``` Notice that every row in `gdp` falls on the first day of a quarter (`01-01`, `04-01`, `07-01`, `10-01`), while `fred` has a row for every month. This means there are two reasonable ways to merge the two datasets, and they produce different results. **Approach A: merge on `DATE` directly.** 1. Merge `fred` with the `DATE` and `GDP` columns from `gdp`, using an inner join on `DATE`. Assign the result to `merged_a` and print its shape. How many rows does it have, and which months survived the merge? Why? **Approach B: merge on year and quarter.** 2. Add `year` and `quarter` columns to both `fred` and `gdp` using the `.dt` accessor. Merge `fred` with the `year`, `quarter`, and `GDP` columns from `gdp`, using a left join on `["year", "quarter"]`. Assign the result to `merged_b` and print its shape. How does it compare to `merged_a`? **Comparing the two approaches.** 3. Print the first 6 rows of both `merged_a` and `merged_b`. In your own words, explain the difference between the two results: what does each row represent in each case? 4. Suppose you want to track the unemployment rate month by month throughout the 2010s while always having the corresponding quarter's GDP available alongside it. Which of the two merged DataFrames is the right one to use for this, and why? 5. Using the DataFrame you identified in step 4, compare the unemployment rate and GDP at the start of the decade to the end. By how many percentage points did unemployment fall? By what percentage did GDP grow?
Solution ::: {#351d4415 .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd url_fred = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv" url_gdp = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/GDP.csv" fred = pd.read_csv(url_fred) fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"]) gdp = pd.read_csv(url_gdp) gdp["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(gdp["DATE"]) # 1. Approach A: merge on DATE directly (inner join) merged_a = fred.merge(gdp[["DATE", "GDP"]], on="DATE", how="inner") print(merged_a.shape) print(merged_a[["DATE", "GDP"]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (40, 7) DATE GDP 0 2010-01-01 16582.7 1 2010-04-01 16743.2 2 2010-07-01 16872.3 3 2010-10-01 16960.9 4 2011-01-01 16920.6 5 2011-04-01 17035.1 6 2011-07-01 17031.3 7 2011-10-01 17222.6 8 2012-01-01 17367.0 9 2012-04-01 17444.5 10 2012-07-01 17469.6 11 2012-10-01 17489.9 12 2013-01-01 17662.4 13 2013-04-01 17709.7 14 2013-07-01 17860.4 15 2013-10-01 18016.1 16 2014-01-01 17954.0 17 2014-04-01 18185.9 18 2014-07-01 18406.9 19 2014-10-01 18500.0 20 2015-01-01 18666.6 21 2015-04-01 18782.2 22 2015-07-01 18857.4 23 2015-10-01 18892.2 24 2016-01-01 19001.7 25 2016-04-01 19062.7 26 2016-07-01 19197.9 27 2016-10-01 19304.4 28 2017-01-01 19398.3 29 2017-04-01 19506.9 30 2017-07-01 19660.8 31 2017-10-01 19882.4 32 2018-01-01 20044.1 33 2018-04-01 20150.5 34 2018-07-01 20276.2 35 2018-10-01 20304.9 36 2019-01-01 20431.6 37 2019-04-01 20602.3 38 2019-07-01 20843.3 39 2019-10-01 20985.4 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#c889467a .cell execution_count=24} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Approach B: merge on year and quarter (left join) fred["year"] = fred["DATE"].dt.year fred["quarter"] = fred["DATE"].dt.quarter gdp["year"] = gdp["DATE"].dt.year gdp["quarter"] = gdp["DATE"].dt.quarter merged_b = fred.merge(gdp[["year", "quarter", "GDP"]], on=["year", "quarter"], how="left") print(merged_b.shape) print(merged_b[["DATE", "GDP"]].head(8)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (120, 9) DATE GDP 0 2010-01-01 16582.7 1 2010-02-01 16582.7 2 2010-03-01 16582.7 3 2010-04-01 16743.2 4 2010-05-01 16743.2 5 2010-06-01 16743.2 6 2010-07-01 16872.3 7 2010-08-01 16872.3 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#21693b56 .cell execution_count=25} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Compare the two print(merged_a[["DATE", "GDP", "UNRATE"]].head(6)) print(merged_b[["DATE", "GDP", "UNRATE"]].head(6)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` DATE GDP UNRATE 0 2010-01-01 16582.7 9.8 1 2010-04-01 16743.2 9.9 2 2010-07-01 16872.3 9.4 3 2010-10-01 16960.9 9.4 4 2011-01-01 16920.6 9.1 5 2011-04-01 17035.1 9.1 DATE GDP UNRATE 0 2010-01-01 16582.7 9.8 1 2010-02-01 16582.7 9.8 2 2010-03-01 16582.7 9.9 3 2010-04-01 16743.2 9.9 4 2010-05-01 16743.2 9.6 5 2010-06-01 16743.2 9.4 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#64dd7ae7 .cell execution_count=26} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 5. Compare UNRATE and GDP start = merged_b.iloc[0] end = merged_b.iloc[-1] unemployment_change = end["UNRATE"] - start["UNRATE"] gdp_growth = (end["GDP"] - start["GDP"]) / start["GDP"] * 100 print(f"Unemployment change: {unemployment_change:.1f} percentage points") print(f"GDP growth: {gdp_growth:.1f}%") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Unemployment change: -6.2 percentage points GDP growth: 26.5% ``` ::: ::: 1. `merged_a` has only 40 rows — one for every quarter in the decade, since an inner join on `DATE` only keeps rows where the exact date matches in both DataFrames. Only January, April, July, and October survive; the other eight months of each year are dropped entirely because `gdp` has no row dated, for example, `2010-02-01`. 2. `merged_b` has 120 rows — one for every month in the decade, since the left join keeps every row from `fred` regardless of whether it falls on a quarter boundary. Months that are not the start of a quarter (February, March, May, June, and so on) now have the same GDP value as the first month of their quarter, since GDP is constant within a quarter and is simply broadcast across all three months. 3. In `merged_a`, each row represents one full quarter, identified by its first month. In `merged_b`, each row represents one month, with the quarter's GDP value repeated for every month that falls within it. Neither is "more correct" — they represent the same underlying data at two different levels of granularity. 4. `merged_b` is the right choice. Since the goal is to track unemployment *month by month*, you need a row for every month — which only `merged_b` provides. `merged_a` would silently drop two out of every three months, making it impossible to see the monthly pattern in unemployment at all. 5. Unemployment fell from 9.8% in January 2010 to 3.6% in December 2019 — a decline of 6.2 percentage points, reflecting the long economic recovery following the 2008 financial crisis. Over the same period, GDP grew by roughly 27%, consistent with a full decade of sustained, if at times slow, economic expansion.
--- ## 🔴 Challenge ### Exercise 3.11.9 This exercise introduces `resample()` — a method for aggregating time series data to a coarser time resolution. It is the time-series equivalent of `groupby()`. To use `resample()`, the DataFrame must have a datetime column set as the index. ```python import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) flights = flights.set_index("date").sort_index() ``` 1. Look up the documentation for `pd.DataFrame.resample()` by running `help(pd.DataFrame.resample)` or searching online. What does the `rule=` parameter control? What are some common rule strings for monthly, quarterly, and annual resampling? 2. Use `resample()` to compute the total passengers per year. Compare the result to the annual totals we computed using `groupby()` in video 3.9 — are they identical? 3. Use `resample()` to compute the total passengers per quarter. Which quarter across all years had the single highest passenger total? 4. Compute the year-on-year percentage change in annual passenger totals using the result from step 2 and `pct_change()`. In which year was the growth rate highest? 5. `resample()` can also be used to go the other way — **upsampling** from a coarser to a finer resolution. Use `resample("D")` on the monthly `passengers` Series to create a daily-resolution version of the data. Use `.asfreq()` instead of `.sum()` — this keeps the existing values at their original dates without aggregating, and introduces `NaN` for the dates that did not exist in the original data. How many missing values are introduced? 6. Assume that passengers are distributed evenly across the days within each month, so that each day's estimated value is the monthly total divided by the number of days in that month. To compute this: first use `.ffill()` to propagate each month's total to every day in that month, then divide by the number of days in each month — you can get this using `.dt.days_in_month` on the index — to obtain the estimated daily passenger count.
Hint Common rule strings for `resample()`: `"YE"` for year-end, `"QE"` for quarter-end, `"ME"` for month-end. After resampling, call `.sum()` or `.mean()` just as you would after `groupby()`. Use `reset_index()` to convert the result back to a regular DataFrame.
Solution ::: {#c4fdc6b0 .cell execution_count=27} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime( flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"], format="%Y-%b" ) flights = flights.set_index("date").sort_index() # 2. Annual totals with resample annual = flights["passengers"].resample("YE").sum().reset_index() annual.columns = ["date", "total_passengers"] print(annual) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` date total_passengers 0 1949-12-31 1520 1 1950-12-31 1676 2 1951-12-31 2042 3 1952-12-31 2364 4 1953-12-31 2700 5 1954-12-31 2867 6 1955-12-31 3408 7 1956-12-31 3939 8 1957-12-31 4421 9 1958-12-31 4572 10 1959-12-31 5140 11 1960-12-31 5714 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#73592708 .cell execution_count=28} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Quarterly totals quarterly = flights["passengers"].resample("QE").sum().reset_index() quarterly.columns = ["date", "total_passengers"] print(quarterly.sort_values("total_passengers", ascending=False).head(5)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` date total_passengers 46 1960-09-30 1736 42 1959-09-30 1570 45 1960-06-30 1468 38 1958-09-30 1400 34 1957-09-30 1336 ``` ::: ::: 2. The annual totals from `resample("YE")` are identical to those from `groupby(flights["date"].dt.year)`. The two approaches differ in syntax and flexibility — `resample()` is more natural for regularly spaced time series and integrates directly with the datetime index, while `groupby()` with `.dt` components works with any DataFrame regardless of whether the date is the index. 3. The highest single-quarter total occurs in Q3 of the final years of the dataset, reflecting both the summer peak and the overall upward trend in passenger numbers throughout the 1950s. 4. The highest year-on-year growth rate occurs in the early years of the dataset, when the base was low and commercial aviation was expanding rapidly. ::: {#d23aab1b .cell execution_count=29} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Year-on-year growth rate annual["pct_change"] = annual["total_passengers"].pct_change() * 100 print(annual[["date", "total_passengers", "pct_change"]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` date total_passengers pct_change 0 1949-12-31 1520 NaN 1 1950-12-31 1676 10.263158 2 1951-12-31 2042 21.837709 3 1952-12-31 2364 15.768854 4 1953-12-31 2700 14.213198 5 1954-12-31 2867 6.185185 6 1955-12-31 3408 18.869899 7 1956-12-31 3939 15.580986 8 1957-12-31 4421 12.236608 9 1958-12-31 4572 3.415517 10 1959-12-31 5140 12.423447 11 1960-12-31 5714 11.167315 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#27713651 .cell execution_count=30} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 5. Upsample to daily resolution with asfreq() daily = flights["passengers"].resample("D").asfreq() print(daily.isna().sum()) print(daily.head(10)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 4209 date 1949-01-01 112.0 1949-01-02 NaN 1949-01-03 NaN 1949-01-04 NaN 1949-01-05 NaN 1949-01-06 NaN 1949-01-07 NaN 1949-01-08 NaN 1949-01-09 NaN 1949-01-10 NaN Freq: D, Name: passengers, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#5941262d .cell execution_count=31} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 6. Spread monthly totals evenly across days daily_filled = flights["passengers"].resample("D").ffill() days_in_month = daily_filled.index.days_in_month daily_estimate = (daily_filled / days_in_month).round(2) print(daily_estimate.head(35)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` date 1949-01-01 3.61 1949-01-02 3.61 1949-01-03 3.61 1949-01-04 3.61 1949-01-05 3.61 1949-01-06 3.61 1949-01-07 3.61 1949-01-08 3.61 1949-01-09 3.61 1949-01-10 3.61 1949-01-11 3.61 1949-01-12 3.61 1949-01-13 3.61 1949-01-14 3.61 1949-01-15 3.61 1949-01-16 3.61 1949-01-17 3.61 1949-01-18 3.61 1949-01-19 3.61 1949-01-20 3.61 1949-01-21 3.61 1949-01-22 3.61 1949-01-23 3.61 1949-01-24 3.61 1949-01-25 3.61 1949-01-26 3.61 1949-01-27 3.61 1949-01-28 3.61 1949-01-29 3.61 1949-01-30 3.61 1949-01-31 3.61 1949-02-01 4.21 1949-02-02 4.21 1949-02-03 4.21 1949-02-04 4.21 Freq: D, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: 5. Upsampling with `.asfreq()` creates a row for every single day between the first and last date in the index, but only the original month-start dates have a value — every other day is `NaN`. This is the gap-filling behavior in action: `resample()` builds its date range from the calendar, not from the rows that happen to exist, so it makes the resolution mismatch between the original data and the daily target explicit rather than silently ignoring it. `groupby()` could not do this at all, since there is nothing in the original data to group by day. 6. `.ffill()` propagates each month's total forward to every day until the next known value, so every day in a given month temporarily holds that month's total. Dividing by `.dt.days_in_month` then converts each day's value from "the whole month's total" to "this day's share of the total," correctly accounting for the fact that months have different lengths (28–31 days). The result is a daily series whose values sum back up to the original monthly totals — a reasonable estimate given the assumption of even distribution, even though the true day-to-day pattern (e.g. weekday vs weekend travel) is not recoverable from monthly data alone.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.10 This exercise is a complete wrangling workflow on the gapminder dataset — a dataset covering GDP per capita, life expectancy, and population for countries around the world from 1952 to 2007. It mirrors the structure of the Topic 3 capstone but on a dataset you have not used before. ```python import pandas as pd url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv" gapminder = pd.read_csv(url) ``` Work through the following steps: 1. **Inspect** the dataset. How many rows and columns does it have? What are the column names and dtypes? Are there any missing values? 2. **Standardise** the column names to snake_case (all lowercase, spaces replaced with underscores). 3. **Filter** to the years 1952 and 2007 only. Create two separate DataFrames — `gapminder_1952` and `gapminder_2007` — and merge them on `country` and `continent` to get both years' values side by side. Use suffixes `_1952` and `_2007` to distinguish the columns. 4. **Compute** two new columns on the merged DataFrame: - `life_exp_growth`: the change in life expectancy from 1952 to 2007 - `gdp_growth`: the percentage change in GDP per capita from 1952 to 2007 5. **Summarise** by continent: compute the mean `life_exp_growth` and mean `gdp_growth` for each continent. Sort by `life_exp_growth` in descending order. 6. **Identify** the top 5 and bottom 5 countries by `life_exp_growth`. Do the countries with the greatest improvements also tend to have the highest GDP growth?
Solution ::: {#c5efc347 .cell execution_count=32} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv" gapminder = pd.read_csv(url) # 1. Inspect print(gapminder.shape) print(gapminder.dtypes) print(gapminder.isna().sum()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (1704, 6) country str year int64 pop float64 continent str lifeExp float64 gdpPercap float64 dtype: object country 0 year 0 pop 0 continent 0 lifeExp 0 gdpPercap 0 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#6822c5a6 .cell execution_count=33} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Standardise column names gapminder.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in gapminder.columns] print(gapminder.columns.tolist()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ['country', 'year', 'pop', 'continent', 'lifeexp', 'gdppercap'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {#abdfce17 .cell execution_count=34} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Filter to 1952 and 2007 and merge gapminder_1952 = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 1952].copy() gapminder_2007 = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 2007].copy() merged = gapminder_1952.merge( gapminder_2007, on=["country", "continent"], suffixes=("_1952", "_2007") ) print(merged.shape) print(merged.head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (142, 10) country year_1952 pop_1952 continent lifeexp_1952 gdppercap_1952 \ 0 Afghanistan 1952 8425333.0 Asia 28.801 779.445314 1 Albania 1952 1282697.0 Europe 55.230 1601.056136 2 Algeria 1952 9279525.0 Africa 43.077 2449.008185 3 Angola 1952 4232095.0 Africa 30.015 3520.610273 4 Argentina 1952 17876956.0 Americas 62.485 5911.315053 year_2007 pop_2007 lifeexp_2007 gdppercap_2007 0 2007 31889923.0 43.828 974.580338 1 2007 3600523.0 76.423 5937.029526 2 2007 33333216.0 72.301 6223.367465 3 2007 12420476.0 42.731 4797.231267 4 2007 40301927.0 75.320 12779.379640 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#d35ae4f0 .cell execution_count=35} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Compute growth columns merged["life_exp_growth"] = ( merged["lifeexp_2007"] - merged["lifeexp_1952"] ).round(2) merged["gdp_growth"] = ( (merged["gdppercap_2007"] - merged["gdppercap_1952"]) / merged["gdppercap_1952"] * 100 ).round(1) ``` ::: ::: {#573459bb .cell execution_count=36} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 5. Summarise by continent print( merged.groupby("continent") .agg( mean_life_exp_growth=("life_exp_growth", "mean"), mean_gdp_growth=("gdp_growth", "mean") ) .round(2) .sort_values("mean_life_exp_growth", ascending=False) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean_life_exp_growth mean_gdp_growth continent Asia 24.41 514.56 Americas 20.33 176.28 Africa 15.67 178.29 Europe 13.24 372.42 Oceania 11.46 190.80 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#ec2dca37 .cell execution_count=37} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 6. Top 5 and bottom 5 countries by life expectancy growth print("Top 5:") print( merged.sort_values("life_exp_growth", ascending=False) [["country", "continent", "life_exp_growth", "gdp_growth"]] .head(5) ) print("\nBottom 5:") print( merged.sort_values("life_exp_growth") [["country", "continent", "life_exp_growth", "gdp_growth"]] .head(5) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Top 5: country continent life_exp_growth gdp_growth 96 Oman Asia 38.06 1120.6 137 Vietnam Asia 33.84 303.5 59 Indonesia Asia 33.18 372.3 109 Saudi Arabia Asia 32.90 235.2 75 Libya Africa 31.23 405.0 Bottom 5: country continent life_exp_growth gdp_growth 141 Zimbabwe Africa -4.96 15.4 121 Swaziland Africa -1.79 293.0 140 Zambia Africa 0.35 10.8 73 Lesotho Africa 0.45 425.1 13 Botswana Africa 3.11 1376.6 ``` ::: ::: **Task 1:** The dataset has 1704 rows and 6 columns with no missing values. Each row represents a country-year observation. **Task 5:** Asia shows the greatest mean improvement in both life expectancy and GDP — driven by countries like Japan, South Korea, and China. Europe shows more modest gains because life expectancy and GDP were already high in 1952. **Task 6:** The countries with the greatest life expectancy improvements are not always those with the highest GDP growth — reflecting that improvements in public health, sanitation, and medicine can drive life expectancy gains independently of economic growth. The bottom 5 countries tend to be those affected by conflict or epidemics, which caused life expectancy to stagnate or fall in parts of sub-Saharan Africa.
--- ### Exercise 3.11.11 This exercise is a multi-step data wrangling challenge that combines joining, string manipulation, missing value handling, and groupby on the Titanic data. The `Name` column contains more information than it first appears — last name, title, and for married women, a maiden name in parentheses — and extracting these cleanly requires some careful string work. Load both Titanic datasets from GitHub and work through the steps below. ```python import pandas as pd url_core = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv" url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv" core = pd.read_csv(url_core) names = pd.read_csv(url_names) print(names["Name"].head(10).tolist()) ``` 1. Prepare `names` for merging: rename `PassengerId`, `Pclass`, and `Sex` to `passenger_id`, `pclass`, and `sex`. Then merge the two DataFrames on all three shared keys using a left join. Verify the row count is 891. 2. The `Name` column follows the format `"Last, Title. Rest"` (e.g. `"Braund, Mr. Owen Harris"`). Use the `.str` accessor to extract: - `last_name`: everything before the comma - `title`: the word between the comma-space and the following period 3. Use `.value_counts()` on `title`. You will see a long tail of rare titles (`"Don"`, `"Lady"`, `"Capt"`, and so on). Create a simplified `title_group` column using `.replace()` or `np.select()` with the following mapping: - `"Mr"` → `"Mr"` - `"Miss"` and `"Mrs"` → `"Ms"` - All other titles → `"Other"` 4. Use the `last_name` column to answer two questions: - Which starting letter is the most common among last names? - What is the most common last name length, measured in number of characters? Compute the length of each last name, then find the most frequent value. 5. Compute the survival rate for each combination of `title_group` and `pclass`. Which title-class combination had the highest survival rate?
Hint For step 2, recall the pattern from earlier exercises: splitting the name on `", "` isolates the part starting with the title, and splitting that result on `"."` extracts just the title word. For `last_name`, you only need a single split on `","` and take the first part. For step 3, `.replace()` takes a dictionary mapping old values to new values, for example `{"Don": "Other", "Lady": "Other"}`. Rather than typing out every rare title individually, you can build this dictionary automatically using a dictionary comprehension over the titles you want to group as `"Other"`: ```python {t: "Other" for t in merged["title"].unique() if t not in ["Mr", "Miss", "Mrs"]} ```
Solution ::: {#73d48511 .cell execution_count=38} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd url_core = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv" url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv" core = pd.read_csv(url_core) names = pd.read_csv(url_names) # 1. Prepare and merge names = names.rename(columns={ "PassengerId": "passenger_id", "Pclass": "pclass", "Sex": "sex" }) merged = core.merge(names, on=["passenger_id", "pclass", "sex"], how="left") print("Rows:", len(merged)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 891 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#b48380f5 .cell execution_count=39} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Extract last_name and title merged["last_name"] = merged["Name"].str.split(",").str[0] merged["title"] = ( merged["Name"].str.split(", ").str[1] .str.split(".").str[0] ) print(merged[["Name", "last_name", "title"]].head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Name last_name title 0 Braund, Mr. Owen Harris Braund Mr 1 Cumings, Mrs. John Bradley (Florence Briggs Th... Cumings Mrs 2 Heikkinen, Miss. Laina Heikkinen Miss 3 Futrelle, Mrs. Jacques Heath (Lily May Peel) Futrelle Mrs 4 Allen, Mr. William Henry Allen Mr ``` ::: ::: ::: {#ab9f656d .cell execution_count=40} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Simplified title group merged["title_group"] = merged["title"].replace( {t: "Other" for t in merged["title"].unique() if t not in ["Mr", "Miss", "Mrs"]} ) merged["title_group"] = merged["title_group"].replace({"Miss": "Ms", "Mrs": "Ms"}) print(merged["title_group"].value_counts()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` title_group Mr 517 Ms 307 Other 67 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#b99ef94a .cell execution_count=41} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Most common starting letter and length of last names print(merged["last_name"].str[0].value_counts().head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` last_name S 86 M 74 B 72 C 69 H 69 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#30e2d450 .cell execution_count=42} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(merged["last_name"].str.len().value_counts().head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` last_name 6 207 7 193 5 130 8 103 4 92 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#e1edb25b .cell execution_count=43} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 5. Survival rate by title group and class print( merged.groupby(["title_group", "pclass"])["survived"] .mean() .round(3) .sort_values(ascending=False) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` title_group pclass Ms 1 0.966 2 0.920 Other 1 0.667 2 0.556 Ms 3 0.500 Other 3 0.393 Mr 1 0.346 3 0.113 2 0.088 Name: survived, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: **Task 2:** Splitting on `", "` first isolates the part of the string starting with the title, and splitting that on `"."` extracts just the title word. This relies on the title always being the second comma-separated segment in the name, which holds for every row in this dataset. **Task 4:** The most common starting letter for last names is "S", followed closely by "M" and "B" — a pattern that broadly reflects the most common surname initials among the British and Northern European passengers who make up most of the dataset. The most common last name length is 6 or 7 characters, which is typical for English-language surnames; very short (3-4 character) and very long (10+ character) names are comparatively rare. **Task 5:** First-class "Ms" (Miss/Mrs) passengers had the highest survival rate — consistent with the "women and children first" pattern combined with the class advantage.
### Exercise 3.11.12 This exercise practices importing and combining multiple files with a for loop. The data comes from FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) and is split into one CSV file per decade, each containing the same five monthly variables. The `CPI` column is the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers, indexed so that the average over 1982–1984 equals 100. The monthly csv files can be found in this Github [folder](https://github.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/tree/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED). ```python import pandas as pd base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/" decades = [1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010] ``` 1. Use a for loop over `decades` to build the file name for each decade, load it from `base_url`, and store each resulting DataFrame in a list called `fred_dfs`. Print the shape of each DataFrame as you load it. 2. Use `pd.concat()` to combine the list into a single DataFrame called `fred`. Reset the index, convert the `DATE` column to datetime and sort the data chronologically. Print the shape of the combined DataFrame and confirm it covers 1950 through 2019. 3. Compute the mean `CPI` for each year using `groupby`. Then compute the year-on-year percentage change in this annual mean using `.pct_change()` — this gives you the annual inflation rate. Multiply by 100 and round to one decimal place. 4. Identify the 5 years with the highest inflation rate and the 5 years with the lowest (most negative) inflation rate. What historical periods do these correspond to? 5. Compute the mean annual inflation rate for each decade. Which decade had the highest average inflation?
Hint For step 1, build each URL inside the loop using an f-string, for example `base_url + f"FRED_monthly_{decade}.csv"`, then pass it directly to `pd.read_csv()`. Remember to use `.append()` to add each DataFrame to `fred_dfs`. For step 5, you can extract the decade from the year using integer arithmetic: `(year // 10) * 10` rounds any year down to its decade, for example `1987` becomes `1980`.
Solution ::: {#a5b2ecb7 .cell execution_count=44} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/FRED/" decades = [1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010] # 1. Import each decade's file in a loop fred_dfs = [] for decade in decades: url = base_url + f"FRED_monthly_{decade}.csv" df = pd.read_csv(url) fred_dfs.append(df) print(decade, df.shape) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 1950 (120, 6) 1960 (120, 6) 1970 (120, 6) 1980 (120, 6) 1990 (120, 6) 2000 (120, 6) 2010 (120, 6) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#7e0901c6 .cell execution_count=45} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 2. Combine, convert, and sort fred = pd.concat(fred_dfs).reset_index(drop=True) fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"]) fred = fred.sort_values("DATE").reset_index(drop=True) print(fred.shape) print(fred["DATE"].min(), "to", fred["DATE"].max()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (840, 6) 1950-01-01 00:00:00 to 2019-12-01 00:00:00 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#049c9d8a .cell execution_count=46} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 3. Annual mean CPI and year-on-year inflation rate annual_cpi = fred.groupby(fred["DATE"].dt.year)["CPI"].mean() inflation = (annual_cpi.pct_change() * 100).round(1) print(inflation.head(10)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` DATE 1950 NaN 1951 8.0 1952 2.3 1953 0.8 1954 0.3 1955 -0.2 1956 1.5 1957 3.4 1958 2.7 1959 1.0 Name: CPI, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#313b6b10 .cell execution_count=47} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 4. Highest and lowest inflation years print("Highest inflation years:") print(inflation.sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)) print("\nLowest inflation years:") print(inflation.sort_values().head(5)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Highest inflation years: DATE 1980 13.5 1979 11.3 1974 11.0 1981 10.4 1975 9.1 Name: CPI, dtype: float64 Lowest inflation years: DATE 2009 -0.3 1955 -0.2 2015 0.1 1954 0.3 1953 0.8 Name: CPI, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#b9a7beb8 .cell execution_count=48} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 5. Mean inflation rate by decade decade_of_year = (inflation.index // 10) * 10 print(inflation.groupby(decade_of_year).mean().round(2)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` DATE 1950 2.20 1960 2.33 1970 7.10 1980 5.56 1990 2.99 2000 2.58 2010 1.76 Name: CPI, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: 4. The highest inflation years cluster in the mid-to-late 1970s and around 1980 — the period of the oil price shocks and the resulting stagflation, when annual inflation exceeded 10%. The lowest (most negative) values appear around 2009, reflecting the brief period of deflation during the global financial crisis, when prices fell rather than rose. 5. The 1970s has by far the highest average inflation rate of any decade in the data, consistent with the well-known "Great Inflation" period in US economic history. The 1950s and 2010s show the lowest average inflation, reflecting relatively stable price growth in both the early postwar period and the decade following the 2008 financial crisis.
